title: Podcast with Corey O’Meara, Chief Quantum Scientist at E.ON Digital Technology - Quantum Computing Report
gist: Corey O’Meara, Chief Quantum Scientist at E.ON, explains how a five-person quantum team screens business problems for genuine classical bottlenecks and claims a peer-to-peer energy-trading project delivered one of the first business-relevant demonstrations of quantum scaling advantage on a D-Wave annealer, while arguing cloud access to quantum hardware will suffice for decades barring real-time grid needs.

themes.theme: Use-case screening: complexity over data volume

insights.claim: E.ON only pursues combinatorial optimization problems where existing literature or its own analysis proves the instances scale with exponential runtime under classical solvers like Gurobi, treating provable classical hardness as a prerequisite for even trying quantum.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A disciplined screen investors can apply to any enterprise quantum claim: no proven classical exponential bottleneck means the quantum pilot is likely theater.
insights.quote: we only really tackle combinatorical optimization problems if we can show or there’s known literature which shows that these problems are hard enough and they scale with exponential runtime using let’s say Gurobi
insights.locator: Yeah, exactly. we I mean for any use case

insights.claim: The deciding factor for quantum suitability is problem complexity, not data volume — a problem needing 10,000 or a million variables before it gets hard is a non-fit, so the team targets problems that are hard at small scale to push today’s machines.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Reframes 'big data' hype: near-term quantum value lives only in small-but-complex instances, narrowing the addressable market sharply.
insights.quote: So in a sense it’s not about big data but about complex data.
insights.locator: So in a sense it’s not about big data

insights.claim: For quantum machine learning, E.ON's trigger is not complexity theory but whether current state-of-the-art classical ML already struggles, at which point hybrid quantum-classical augmentation becomes a candidate, citing research suggesting provable quantum advantages from such hybrids.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A second, distinct selection heuristic for QML pilots — watch for hybrid-advantage claims tied to already-failing classical baselines.
insights.locator: Complex data in terms of optimization and also quantum machine learning

themes.theme: Peer-to-peer energy trading and the quantum scaling-advantage claim

insights.claim: E.ON, with startup Aqarios (Aquarius GmbH), LMU Munich, University of Oxford and DFKI, demonstrated evidence of quantum scaling advantage on a D-Wave annealer for prosumer coalition formation, published in IOP's QST journal roughly two-to-three months before the interview.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A datable, peer-reviewed enterprise quantum-advantage claim to verify — the kind of milestone that moves the D-Wave/annealing narrative.
insights.quote: we actually proved a kind of quantum or let’s prove this is a strong word. Let’s say we demonstrated evidence of quantum scaling advantage for running this on a D-Wave quantum computer
insights.locator: Sure, yeah. So in terms of the coalition formation

insights.claim: O’Meara frames the result as the first real-world, business-relevant evidence of quantum scaling advantage, distinguishing it from prior J.P. Morgan (Science Advances, LABS problem) and USC (PRL) demonstrations that were mathematically interesting but not grounded in a real application.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Positions E.ON's work as a category-first business milestone — a marketing wedge but also a testable priority claim against JPM/USC precedents.
insights.quote: if I were to sell it a little bit here I would say it’s you know the first real-world business relevant application and proof or evidence of quantum scaling advantage
insights.locator: so we’re quite proud of that result

insights.claim: The coalition-formation method reuses a DFKI algorithm that sequentially applies MaxCut in a hybrid quantum-classical workflow, tuned by E.ON onto real-world prosumer graphs rather than synthetic benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Shows the near-term playbook is hybrid decomposition of hard graph problems, not monolithic quantum algorithms — relevant to where value actually accrues.
insights.quote: they developed a nice algorithm for essentially sequentially applying MaxCut algorithm. So it’s a hybrid workflow.
insights.locator: So we knew some collaborators from, also in Germany, from DFKI

insights.claim: Optimally grouping prosumers to locally balance decentralized solar, battery and EV generation is an exponentially scaling problem whose classical heuristics also scale poorly, which is why E.ON sees it as a genuine quantum candidate rather than hype.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Identifies decentralized-energy coordination as a concrete commercial vertical where quantum could unlock a new business model.
insights.quote: This scales– this is exponential scaling if you try to run this on classical computers. There’s heuristics for it, which also scale poorly.
insights.locator: so finding out the best way to group prosumers

themes.theme: Hardware access strategy: cloud, ownership and real-time grid windows

insights.claim: O’Meara expects cloud access to quantum computers to remain sufficient for the foreseeable future measured in decades, since these are large specialized shared machines, rather than enterprises needing to own hardware.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Bullish for quantum-cloud/QCaaS providers over on-prem hardware sales as the enterprise access model for the next decade-plus.
insights.quote: with the cloud access, the way you access it these days, I think probably fine for the foreseeable future in the sense of decades
insights.locator: I mean, I would want to own a quantum computer, yes

insights.claim: The energy sector's constant real-time balancing of grid supply and demand imposes a dedicated, fixed runtime window, so unlike weekly logistics scheduling, quantum speedups must fit inside that window — the main scenario that could eventually justify owned hardware and SLAs.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Real-time grid balancing is the specific trigger that would flip the buy-vs-cloud decision and create demand for guaranteed-latency quantum SLAs.
insights.quote: in the energy sector because it’s such a constantly changing dynamic thing it We have our domain itself has a dedicated runtime window so any kind of potential speed up we’re fixed in inside this window
insights.locator: but one thing that’s interesting about our use cases

insights.claim: O’Meara predicts a 'mad rush' by industries to secure access to quantum machines once practical quantum advantage is proven beyond doubt, raising open questions about queuing capacity and how many physical machines can be built.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Implies capacity/queuing could become the binding constraint post-advantage, favoring incumbents with early cloud allocation and first-mover access.
insights.quote: I hope that it’s going to be a mad rush to get access to these machines once It’s really you know once there’s no shadow of a doubt
insights.locator: I often think like what will happen to industries

insights.claim: E.ON signed up with both IBM (27-qubit chips at the time) and D-Wave (5000-qubit Advantage annealer) five years ago, using annealing for optimization and gate-based IBM hardware for quantum machine learning, and reports satisfaction with both providers.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A concrete enterprise dual-vendor split — annealing for optimization, gate-based for ML — signaling both modalities retain enterprise demand.
insights.locator: when we started five years ago at the time I think IBM had 27 qubit chips

insights.claim: O’Meara believes porting E.ON's universal gate-based algorithms to a better competing quantum computer would mostly be compilation-level detail, and the team deliberately keeps code relatively hardware-agnostic to stay portable through the hardware race.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Low switching costs undercut hardware-vendor lock-in for gate-based workloads — relevant to durability of any single hardware vendor's moat.
insights.quote: it’s essentially down to sort of compilation level details I would imagine
insights.locator: How difficult do you anticipate it would be to move this code

themes.theme: Timing, cost economics and ROI framing

insights.claim: E.ON's optimization runs (coalition formation, vehicle-to-grid) execute in minutes not hours on today's hardware, though quantum machine learning with on-device training takes far longer due to repeated runs.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Runtime is already grid-compatible for optimization, so latency is not the near-term blocker — advantage and cost are.
insights.quote: for optimization, it was a matter of minutes
insights.locator: Let me think off the top of my head

insights.claim: O’Meara argues per-hour quantum compute cost (whether $5,000 or $20,000) is largely irrelevant when the underlying grid/energy-trading payoff is large, so use-case development weighs financial gain from speedup, not machine cost.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: In high-value verticals like energy trading, willingness-to-pay for quantum compute is set by the payoff, implying pricing power for advantaged solutions.
insights.locator: if it’s $5,000 or $20,000, if the problem is solved

insights.claim: O’Meara insists a proven scientific quantum advantage is worthless to the business unless it yields monetary value, grid stability, safety or resilience — explicitly separating a 'cool science result' from a fundable business case.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: The commercialization filter that will separate investable quantum applications from academically impressive dead-ends.
insights.quote: that’s just a cool science result and doesn’t really have an effect for the business at the end of the day
insights.locator: Because, you know, just because you can

themes.theme: Building internal quantum capability and first-mover advantage

insights.claim: E.ON's quantum program is a five-person team of physics and quantum-computing PhDs (ex-professors, postdocs) who learn the energy domain only after joining, started around 2020 from a board-level, top-down mandate.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Shows the staffing template for enterprise quantum: hire quantum PhDs, teach them the vertical — a scarce-talent bottleneck for followers.
insights.quote: we’re all PhDs in physics or quantum computing and yeah, so ex-professors, postdocs, etc. And then when they join E.ON, we learn about the energy sector.
insights.locator: We did the first way, so the first order

insights.claim: O’Meara tells peers that even after five years of buildout, a US energy provider starting now may be 'a little bit too late,' stressing that first-mover advantage matters greatly given the difficulty of organizational onboarding and finding where quantum applies.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Frames enterprise quantum readiness as a multi-year capability moat, not a switch to flip later — a timing thesis for incumbents deciding whether to start.
insights.quote: I would say there may be even a little bit too late honestly
insights.locator: When should we get started

insights.claim: O’Meara positions his core internal role as bringing the hype down and managing board expectations, telling leadership there is no ROI in the immediate future and that returns wait years until fault-tolerant hardware arrives.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A credibility signal: the enterprise buyer itself expects no near-term ROI, tempering revenue timelines for quantum vendors selling into utilities.
insights.quote: they know there’s no ROI in the immediate future. We will wait some more years till fault tolerant is here
insights.locator: so they’re aware that fault tolerant is some years away

insights.claim: E.ON has published work on hybrid software architecture integrating Docker containers and Azure cloud deployments, building government-grant projects to anticipate integrating quantum subroutines into existing cloud infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Enterprise quantum adoption runs through mainstream cloud/container stacks (Azure), favoring hyperscalers as the integration layer.
insights.locator: I mean we have some papers out about hybrid software architecture

insights.claim: O’Meara envisions a try-catch-style fail-safe where hard problem instances that time out on classical heuristics get routed to a quantum solver, with a classical fallback accepted only at the cost of losing accuracy since classical answers are approximations.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: The likely production integration pattern for quantum in critical infrastructure — quantum as an escalation path, classical as a lossy backup.
insights.quote: like a try-catch statement almost, right? You’re writing code and then you try it and okay, there’s a timeout limit because maybe that particular instance of the problem is extremely hard
insights.locator: I would imagine if there were some kind of test

themes.theme: Field outlook: fault tolerance and algorithm scarcity

insights.claim: O’Meara now uses 150-156 qubit machines daily and cites credible roadmaps to error-corrected logical qubits at scale running hundreds of millions of gates, noting companies are for the first time putting their name on fault tolerance being within five years rather than the perpetual 'five-to-ten years away.'
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A practitioner signal that fault-tolerance timelines have firmed to roughly five years — the horizon on which utility-scale quantum ROI is being underwritten.
insights.quote: now companies really put their name on the line that it really is within five years
insights.locator: I find the roadmaps default tolerance amazing

insights.claim: O’Meara flags a shortage of quantum algorithms with provable asymptotic advantage as a core field bottleneck, praising Google's decoded quantum interferometry result as the kind of novel algorithm development that is critical because such algorithms are extremely hard to invent.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Algorithm discovery, not just hardware, is the gating input for real quantum advantage — a differentiator to watch among research labs.
insights.quote: the new decoded quantum interferometry result from the Google team. So these novel algorithm developments are absolutely critical for the whole field.
insights.locator: And from a algorithmic perspective

insights.claim: O’Meara deliberately positions himself between hype and skepticism, citing skeptics like Scott Aronson and Gil Kalai as an important counterweight to balance commercial momentum with scientific rigor.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: The insider's calibrated stance — treat both maximalist roadmaps and outright skepticism with caution when underwriting quantum timelines.
insights.quote: I think the skepticism of people like him in the field, I think is very important, especially to balance the hype with the academic and the scientific
insights.locator: I would love to have dinner with a previous guest of yours
